description: 'rotiku' is patterned bodily movement that follows or makes a rhythm. A dancer may follow music, another body, or a remembered sequence; sometimes the movement itself sets the rhythm. 'wile' (play) is wider recreation, while 'rotiku' keeps the bodily pattern at the centre.
articulatory_notes: Tap or briefly trill the tongue at the alveolar ridge for 'r', then draw the tongue back and round the lips into middle-height 'o'. Place the tongue tip against the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k' and release into high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The tongue moves from a tap to a dental stop and then a velar stop while the vowels alternate between rounded and unrounded positions. Those distinct beats give the word a useful rhythmic contour for dance.
usage_notes: 'rotiku' is ordinarily intransitive. A 'lila' frame places an intended outcome before the dancing. An occasion uses 'sui', as in 'sui pharuki' (during a celebration); a preceding adjective describes the dancer's manner. Noun 'rotiku' can be a dance or a single dancing event.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Art Nouveau finds repeated living curves in vines, water, cloth, and the human figure. Dance makes comparable lines in time through the moving body.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A learned dance can carry a community's timing and memory without a written score. 'rotiku' names the embodied act while the particular dance keeps its own lineage.

semantic_domains.activity: participation in dancing as recreation, practice, or performance
semantic_domains.aesthetic: rhythm and bodily form experienced or shaped as a dance
semantic_domains.physical: patterned movement made through the body
semantic_domains.ritual: dance performed as part of a ceremony or repeated observance
